reasoning: Without access to the Polymarket URL or web search, I must estimate the probability based on background climate knowledge up to the knowledge cutoff of October 2025. The market asks if the October 2025 globally averaged temperature anomaly (NASA GISTEMP relative to 1951-1980) will be LESS than 1.10 °C. The world saw an unprecedented spike in temperatures in 2023, with October 2023 reaching 1.34 °C. Early 2024 also consistently remained around 1.20–1.35 °C. While a transition to La Niña could normally be expected to bring anomalies down significantly, the baseline temperature appears to have experienced a structural step-up due to a combination of secular warming, IMO 2020 shipping sulfate reductions (acting forcefully on the North Atlantic), and solar maximum. Previous La Niña floors (e.g., 2021/2022) were near 0.9–1.0 °C. If the recent El Niño peak added ~0.2–0.3 °C above the neutral baseline, and the new neutral baseline has shifted upward relative to the pre-2023 trend, a La Niña or neutral floor in October 2025 is more likely to sit in the 1.10–1.20 °C range rather than falling back below 1.10 °C. Therefore, I assign a lower probability to the temperature dropping completely below 1.10 °C.
